title: Reference Systems-2026
text: Welcome to the Reference Systems-2026 course page. THis course is based on Iliffe, Jonathan , and Roger Lott . 2008. Datums and Map Projections for Remote Sensing, GIS, and Surveying. Whittles Pub. CRC Press, Scotland, UK.

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
section: The Earth is not a mathematical shape
text:
The Earth is not a mathematical shape
The physical surface of the Earth is irregular — mountains, valleys, ocean trenches.
We cannot define coordinates directly on this surface.
We need a simpler model.

Source: https://science.nasa.gov/earth/earth-observatory/blue-marble-next-generation/

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
First approximation — the Geoid
The geoid is the shape the ocean would take under gravity alone — no currents, no wind, no temperature differences.

An equipotential surface of Earth’s gravity field
Irregular — deviates from a smooth shape by ±100 m
Good for defining heights, too complex for defining coordinates


Source: https://www.esa.int/ESA_Multimedia/Images/2011/03/New_GOCE_geoid

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
Second approximation — the Ellipsoid
An ellipsoid (oblate spheroid) — defined by just two parameters.

Semi-major axis \(a\) (equatorial radius)
Inverse flattening \(1/f\)

Smooth, mathematically simple, good enough for horizontal coordinates.

Source: Wikipedia commons

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
Three surfaces — summary

Source: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s12517-024-11884-w?fromPaywallRec=true#Fig1

Physical surface => Real, irregular
Geoid => Gravity-based, bumpy; used for Height reference
Ellipsoid => Mathematical, smooth; Horizontal coordinates

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
What is a coordinate system?
A set of rules for assigning numbers to points.
Purely abstract — no connection to the Earth yet.

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
Attributes of a coordinate system
Every coordinate system defines:

Number of axes — 2 or 3
Axis names — latitude, longitude, easting, X, Y, Z …
Axis directions — north, east, up …
Axis units — degrees, metres, feet …
Axis order — which comes first?

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
Many coordinate systems exist
The same point on Earth can be described using different coordinate systems.
We now look at the main types.

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
Coordinates on a sphere
On a sphere, position is defined by two angles:

Latitude \(\varphi\) — angle from the equatorial plane to a line to the centre
Longitude \(\lambda\) — angle from the prime meridian

On a sphere, the surface normal passes through the centre — latitude has only one definition.

Source: Wikipedia commons

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
Coordinates on an ellipsoid
On an ellipsoid, the surface normal does not pass through the centre.
This creates two different definitions of latitude:

Geodetic latitude — angle of the surface normal to the equatorial plane
Geocentric latitude — angle of the line to the centre to the equatorial plane

They differ by up to ~11.5 arc-minutes (~21 km) at 45°.

Source: Wikipedia commons

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
Ellipsoidal coordinate system



Coordinate
Symbol
Range




Geodetic latitude
\(\varphi\)
−90° to +90°


Geodetic longitude
\(\lambda\)
−180° to +180°


Ellipsoidal height
\(h\)
metres above ellipsoid




2D version: \((\varphi, \lambda)\)
3D version: \((\varphi, \lambda, h)\)


Source: Wikipedia commons

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
Geocentric Cartesian system
Origin at Earth’s centre of mass. Three straight-line axes.



Axis
Points toward




X
Equator ∩ Prime Meridian


Y
Equator ∩ 90°E


Z
North Pole



Units: metres. No ellipsoid needed.
Used in GNSS processing and satellite geodesy.

Source: Wikipedia commons

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
Conversion between ellipsoidal and geocentric
These describe the same point — conversion is exact.
Ellipsoidal → Geocentric: Section 2.2.4 has conversion formulas

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
Projected coordinate systems
Convert curved \((\varphi, \lambda)\) to flat \((E, N)\) — Easting and Northing.

Units: metres
Enables flat maps, distance/area calculations on a plane
Always introduces distortion (shape, area, distance, or direction)

We cover projections in detail in Lectures 4 and 5.

Source: Wikimedia commons

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
Local engineering coordinate systems
For small areas — construction sites, buildings, bridges.

Origin at a convenient local point
Axes: typically East, North, Up
Earth’s curvature ignored (area small enough)
Units: metres

Must be connected to a global CRS to integrate with other data.

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
Gravity-Related Height Systems
GPS gives ellipsoidal height \(h\) — height above the ellipsoid.
We usually want orthometric height \(H\) — height above the geoid (“sea level”).
\[h = H + N\]
where \(N\) is the geoid undulation (geoid height above/below the ellipsoid).

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
What is a datum?
A coordinate system is abstract — floating in mathematical space.
A datum anchors it to the real Earth.
It defines:

Where the origin is
How the axes are oriented
What scale is used

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
Without a datum
Same coordinate system, no anchor → coordinates are meaningless.
With a datum → every number refers to a specific place on Earth.

Source: Iliffe, Jonathan , and Roger Lott . 2008. Datums and Map Projections for Remote Sensing, GIS, and Surveying. Whittles Pub. CRC Press, Scotland, UK.

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
Geodetic datum
A geodetic datum specifically anchors an ellipsoid to the Earth for defining latitude and longitude.
Two ingredients:

Which ellipsoid (shape and size)
How it is positioned and oriented relative to the Earth

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
Before satellites — local datums
Steps to realise a local datum:

Choose an ellipsoid that fits your region well (e.g., Bessel 1841)
Pick a fundamental point on the surface (e.g., Rauenberg, Germany)
Align the ellipsoid so its normal matches gravity direction at that point
Orient the minor axis parallel to Earth’s rotation axis
Survey outward from the fundamental point to build a network

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
Local datums — the result

Fits well near the fundamental point
Gets worse further away
Every country built their own → many datums worldwide


Source: Kalu, I., Ndehedehe, C. E., Okwuashi, O., & Eyoh, A. E. (2022). A comparison of existing transformation models to improve coordinate conversion between geodetic reference frames in Nigeria. Modeling Earth Systems and Environment, 8(1), 611-624.

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
After satellites — geocentric datums
Satellites see the whole Earth → we can locate the centre of mass.
Steps to realise a geocentric datum:

Define the origin (centre of mass), axis directions, and scale
Observe from a global network using VLBI, SLR, GNSS, DORIS
Compute station coordinates as (X, Y, Z) in a consistent global frame
Assign velocities to account for plate tectonics
Optionally associate an ellipsoid (e.g., GRS 80) when users want lat/lon

Result: consistent worldwide, no regional bias.

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
CRS = Coordinate System + Datum
\[\boxed{\text{CRS} = \text{Coordinate System} + \text{Datum}}\]
The coordinate system defines how numbers work.
The datum defines where they point.
Together: unambiguous positions on Earth.

All use ellipsoidal coordinates (latitude °, longitude °) — but different anchors.

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
ITRS — the definition
International Terrestrial Reference System

Origin: Earth’s centre of mass
Z axis: IERS Reference Pole
X axis: IERS Reference Meridian ∩ Equator
Unit: SI metre
Maintained by: IERS

This is a theoretical definition — a set of conventions on paper.

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
ITRF — the realisation
International Terrestrial Reference Frame
The physical realisation: actual coordinates and velocities of real stations.
Built from four observation techniques: VLBI, SLR, GNSS, DORIS

[Source: https://itrf.ign.fr/en/network/map] {.small}

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
ITRF realisations over time



Realisation
Reference epoch
Released




ITRF2000
1997.0
2001


ITRF2005
2000.0
2007


ITRF2008
2005.0
2010


ITRF2014
2010.0
2016


ITRF2020
2015.0
2022



Each includes station velocities — because plates move.
Coordinates are only valid at the reference epoch.

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
ETRS89 — Europe’s solution
Problem: in ITRF, European stations drift ~2.5 cm/year northeast.
Solution: ETRS89 — a reference frame that moves with the Eurasian plate.

Coordinates of European stations stay (nearly) constant over time
Coincided with ITRF at epoch 1989.0
Used by all European national mapping agencies

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
WGS 84



Property
Value




Ellipsoid
\(a\) = 6,378,137 m, \(1/f\) = 298.257223563


Origin
Earth’s centre of mass


Maintained by
US NGA (National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency)


Realised through
GPS satellite orbits + ~17 tracking stations

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
WGS 84 Realisations

Original (1987) — based on Doppler observations, ~1–2 m accuracy
G730 (Jan 1994) — aligned to ITRF91
G873 (Jan 1997) — aligned to ITRF94
G1150 (Jan 2002) — aligned to ITRF2000
G1674 (Feb 2012) — aligned to ITRF2008
G1762 (Oct 2013) — aligned to ITRF2008
G2139 (Jan 2021) — aligned to ITRF2014

The “G” number is the GPS week number when the realisation became operational.

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
section: WGS 84 — how it is tied to ITRF
text:
WGS 84 — how it is tied to ITRF
WGS 84 is periodically re-aligned to the latest ITRF:

NGA computes coordinates of its tracking stations in ITRF
These become the new WGS 84 station coordinates
GPS orbits are computed relative to these stations
Your receiver’s position is effectively in that realisation

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
section: The same lat/lon can mean different places
text:
The same lat/lon can mean different places
The values \(\varphi = 51.969°\), \(\lambda = 7.665°\) are just numbers.
Their meaning depends on:

Which ellipsoid? (Bessel, GRS 80, WGS 84 …)
Which datum? (DHDN, ED50, ETRS89, WGS 84 …)
Which realisation? (WGS 84 G730 vs. G2139 …)
Which epoch? (2010.0 vs. 2025.0 in ITRF …)

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
How much can it shift?
The same physical point — different lat/lon depending on the datum:



From → To
shift




ED50 → WGS 84 (Münster)
118.9 m


NAD27 → NAD83 (New York)
36.4 m


DHDN → ETRS89 (Münster)
163 m


ITRF2014 epoch 2010 → epoch 2025
40 cm (2.5 cm per year)


WGS 84 (1990) → WGS 84 (2024)
~1–2 m (documented)



Without knowing the datum, coordinates are ambiguous.

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
Summary

The Earth is not mathematical → we approximate with geoid and ellipsoid
A coordinate system defines axes, units, directions — but is abstract
A datum anchors the coordinate system to the real Earth
CRS = Coordinate System + Datum — the complete package
Local datums (pre-satellite) → geocentric datums (post-satellite)
ITRF is the most accurate global frame; ETRS89 is Europe’s plate-fixed version
WGS 84 is periodically aligned to ITRF but has multiple realisations
Latitude and longitude are not unique without specifying the CRS

text:

title: Coordinates and Reference Systems
text:
For Lecture 3
Read Chapter 3 of “datums and map projection” (1st edition) and sections 2.3.4 to 2.5.2 of the coursebook
Think about:

Are vertical datums related to horizontal datums? If yes, in which way?
List two different types of height, and name their differences.
What is the difference between the normal and the vertical?
Why could the adoption of a single vertical datum over large distances be problematic for height measurement?

Submit questions to Learnweb by Wednesday noon.

title: Discussion questions
text:
The Earth — Geoid and Ellipsoid

What is the difference between the geoid and the ellipsoid, and how much do they differ?
How is the difference between the geoid and mean sea level at most around 1–2 m?
Does Figure 2.2 depict geoid undulation — the deviation of the geoid from the ellipsoid?
How do coordinate definitions differ between a geoid/sphere and an ellipsoid?
Instead of using slightly flawed models, can’t we just model the whole Earth and make the perfect CRS?

title: Discussion questions
section: The Earth — Geoid and Ellipsoid — Answers
text:
The Earth — Geoid and Ellipsoid — Answers

Geoid vs. ellipsoid: The geoid is an irregular equipotential surface shaped by Earth’s actual gravity field. The ellipsoid is a smooth mathematical approximation defined by just two parameters. They differ by roughly ±100 m globally — the largest deviation is in the Indian Ocean (~−106 m).
Geoid vs. MSL (~1–2 m): MSL is the observed average ocean surface, which is affected by permanent currents, temperature, and salinity. The geoid is where water would rest under gravity alone. The permanent deviation between them is called Mean Dynamic Topography — roughly ±1–2 m.
Figure 2.2: Yes — it shows geoid undulation \(N\), the height of the geoid above or below the reference ellipsoid at each location.
Coordinate definitions on geoid vs. sphere vs. ellipsoid: On a sphere, latitude is simply the angle from the equatorial plane to the centre. On an ellipsoid, geodetic latitude is the angle between the surface normal and the equatorial plane — these are not the same because the normal does not pass through the centre. The geoid is too irregular to define coordinates on directly, which is why we use the ellipsoid.
Why not model the whole Earth perfectly? We do — that’s the geoid. But the geoid is too complex for coordinate calculations. The ellipsoid is a deliberate simplification that makes mathematics tractable. The difference between them is handled separately through geoid models.

title: Discussion questions
text:
Coordinate Systems — Attributes and Conventions

Can coordinate systems be reasonably interpreted even if not all attributes are given?
Is axis order constrained by the order in which coordinates were recorded? What happens if axes are swapped?
What is meant by the “ellipsoidal nominal”?
In which country are grads used instead of degrees, and why?

title: Discussion questions
section: Coordinate Systems — Attributes and Conventions — Answers
text:
Coordinate Systems — Attributes and Conventions — Answers

Incomplete attributes: Sometimes software makes assumptions (e.g., “degrees” and “lat first”), but these assumptions vary between tools. Without explicit documentation of axes, units, and order, interpretation is ambiguous — you might get lucky.
Axis order: Axis order is defined by the CRS standard, not by how data was recorded. ISO 19111 says geographic CRS axes are (latitude, longitude), but GeoJSON and many tools use (longitude, latitude).
Ellipsoidal normal: This likely refers to the normal to the ellipsoid — the line perpendicular to the ellipsoid surface at a given point. Geodetic latitude is defined as the angle this normal makes with the equatorial plane.
Grads: France historically used grads (gradians), where a right angle = 100 grad instead of 90°. Some French and Swiss cadastral data still uses grads. A full circle = 400 grad.

title: Discussion questions
section: Coordinates on the Sphere and Ellipsoid
text:
Coordinates on the Sphere and Ellipsoid

What is the difference between geodetic latitude and geocentric latitude? Why are multiple definitions of latitude needed on an ellipsoid?
Can all latitudes be classified as parallels and all longitudes as meridians?
When is it more advantageous to use geocentric Cartesian coordinates instead of geographic coordinates?

title: Discussion questions
section: Coordinates on the Sphere and Ellipsoid — Answers
text:
Coordinates on the Sphere and Ellipsoid — Answers

Geodetic vs. geocentric latitude: Geocentric latitude is the angle from the equatorial plane to a line drawn to the Earth’s centre. Geodetic latitude is the angle from the equatorial plane to the normal to the ellipsoid surface. On a sphere they are identical. On an ellipsoid they differ — by up to ~11.5 minutes of arc (~21 km) at 45° latitude. We need multiple definitions because the ellipsoid’s curvature varies, so the surface normal does not point at the centre.
Parallels and meridians: Yes. Lines of constant latitude are called parallels. Lines of constant longitude are called meridians.
When to use geocentric Cartesian: When computing satellite orbits, GNSS processing, or performing datum transformations (e.g., 7-parameter Helmert). These operations involve rotations and translations in 3D space, which are simple matrix operations in Cartesian (X, Y, Z) but extremely messy in latitude/longitude.

title: Discussion questions
text:
Conversions and Transformations

What is the difference between a coordinate conversion and a coordinate transformation?
How does the conversion between ellipsoidal and geocentric Cartesian coordinates work mathematically?
The book says conversions and transformations are sometimes “intrinsically bundled together.” Can you give a practical example?

title: Discussion questions
text:
Conversions and Transformations — Answers

Conversion vs. transformation: A conversion changes the coordinate system but stays on the same datum — it is exact and reversible (e.g., geographic ↔︎ geocentric, geographic → projected). A transformation changes the datum — it is empirical, based on observations, and always has some error (e.g., ED50 → WGS 84). Key test: if no datum change is involved, it’s a conversion.
Ellipsoidal → geocentric maths: Section 2.2.4 equations 2.6 till 2.8 and 2.9 till 2.14 for the reverse
Bundled conversion + transformation: When you reproject a dataset from, say, ED50 geographic to WGS 84 / UTM zone 32N, the software internally performs a datum transformation (ED50 → WGS 84) and a coordinate conversion (geographic → projected) in a single pipeline. In QGIS or ArcGIS this appears as one operation, but PROJ internally chains them: ED50 latlon → ED50 XYZ → WGS84 XYZ → WGS84 latlon → UTM.

title: Discussion questions
text:
Engineering Coordinates and Gravity-Related Heights

Why would the relationship between two engineering height systems be more complex than a simple offset?
What is the difference between orthometric height, normal height, and geopotential number? -> Leave this for Lecture 3.
What is a vertical coordinate system?
What exactly is the difference between a vertical datum and a geodetic datum?

title: Discussion questions
section: Engineering Coordinates and Gravity-Related Heights — Answers
text:
Engineering Coordinates and Gravity-Related Heights — Answers

More than a simple offset: Two surveyors may use different control points, different instruments, and accumulate different measurement errors. The result is that the height difference between points A and B in system 1 may not match the difference in system 2.
Three height types: Leave this for Lecture 3.
Vertical coordinate system: A 1D coordinate system with a single axis: height (upward) or depth (downward), in metres, referenced to a vertical datum.
Vertical vs. geodetic datum: A geodetic datum anchors an ellipsoid to the Earth for horizontal positions (lat/lon). A vertical datum defines a reference surface for heights — typically the geoid or a MSL measurement at a specific tide gauge. They are independent: you can use ETRS89 (geodetic datum) with DHHN2016 (vertical datum).

title: Discussion questions
text:
Datums

What is the practical process of defining and realising a datum? Can you demonstrate how one is built?
What is the purpose of the datum?
If coordinate transformations between datums can have errors, how do geospatial systems ensure reliability when integrating datasets from multiple sources?

title: Discussion questions
text:
Datums — Answers

Building a (local) datum — step by step: (1) Choose an ellipsoid (e.g., Bessel 1841). (2) Pick a fundamental point on Earth’s surface (e.g., Rauenberg for the German DHDN). (3) At that point, fix the ellipsoid so that the surface normal aligns with the local gravity direction (“deflection of the vertical” = 0 at this point). (4) Orient the minor axis parallel to Earth’s rotation axis. (5) Survey outward from this point to build a network of known coordinates. For a geocentric datum (e.g., ITRF): skip the fundamental point — instead, use satellite observations to locate the Earth’s centre of mass and define the origin there.
Purpose of a datum: It anchors an abstract coordinate system to the physical Earth. Without it, coordinates are just numbers floating in mathematical space with no connection to real locations.
Reliability despite transformation errors: In practice: (1) use the most accurate transformation available; (2) record and propagate accuracy metadata (3) choose transformations appropriate to your area of use; (4) accept and document residual uncertainty. There is no way to eliminate transformation error entirely — the goal is to understand and manage it.

title: Discussion questions
text:
ITRS, WGS 84, and Time

Why do coordinates in the ITRS require a specific time stamp?
What practical problems could arise when two GIS datasets use the same CRS but were created at different points in time?

title: Discussion questions
text:
ITRS, WGS 84, and Time — Answers

Why ITRF needs a time stamp: The Earth’s tectonic plates move continuously — Europe drifts ~2.5 cm/year northeast relative to Earth’s centre. ITRF station coordinates therefore change over time. An ITRF position is only valid at a specific epoch (e.g., 2015.0).
Same CRS, different times: If the CRS is a dynamic datum (like ITRF), the coordinates of the same physical point are literally different at different epochs — so two datasets “in ITRF2014” but at epochs 2015.0 and 2025.0 will show a systematic shift.

title: Reference Systems
text:
2. Learning objectives
By the end of the course you will be able to:

Identify, interpret, and critically evaluate coordinate reference system information in geospatial data and software
Select and apply appropriate projections, datums, and transformations for a given task
Diagnose and fix reference system problems when combining, transforming, or sharing spatial data

title: Reference Systems
text:
2. Learning objectives …
And along the way, you’ll be able to explain:

Why Africa looks the same size as Greenland on many world maps — even though it is 14 times larger -> True size
Why Google Maps uses a projection that is mathematically wrong — and why they chose it anyway
Why many world maps show Antarctica as an infinitely wide strip at the bottom — or simply cut it off
Why your phone’s GPS might say you’re at 8820 m elevation while the official sign at the summit says 8848 m — and both are correct

title: Reference Systems
text:
Summary: Reference systems-definition

Key features of any reference system are:

Origin
Orientation
Unit of Measurement

title: Reference Systems
text:
Summary

Values are not meaningful by themselves, their accurate interpretation necessitates knowledge of the reference system(s)
Put differently: without a documentation of the reference systems, ambiguity is guaranteed
Conversion between values in different reference systems is often desirable, sometimes possible without information loss, sometimes not
Reference systems are not absolute, they are created (and live by) conventions

title: Reference Systems
text: 4. Where/why we need reference systems?

title: Reference Systems
text:
Positioning

GPS uses World Geodetic System 1984 (WGS 84)
GLONASS uses Parametry Zemli (PZ-90)
Galileo uses Galile Terestrial Reference Frame (GTRF)
Beidou uses China Terrestrial Reference Frame (CGCS 2000)
https://gssc.esa.int/navipedia/index.php/Reference_Frames_in_GNSS
Which of these system does your smart phone use?

title: Reference Systems
text:
Course format

Each participant studies the course materials by themselves in-depth, core conepts will be revisited in lecture and discussions
Ask 1 or 2 question (related to the lecture) to be discussed in the class

No questions from you means everything is understood
No question from you means less content for lecture
The more questions we have, the more we all learn
Questions may concern understanding, or bring up a discussion

Labs repeat and complement the discussion in class, and help understand the theory by using it in practice{.smaller}

Schedule of labs: will be distributed in lab class today

title: Reference Systems
text:
Workload

5 CP, 12 weeks (this week included)
5*30/12 ≈ 12.5 - 3 = 9.5 hours per week
Note that this would sum to a study load of 60 hrs /week; since most of the work here concerns reading, consider doing most of it during the first half of the semester. The second half will be more demanding for all other courses.
